feedback: The model demonstrates basic-to-emerging competence in malware analysis, achieving exact match on only 2 of 18 questions (11.1%) but maintaining a reasonable average Jaccard of ~0.627, indicating it identifies most correct options but consistently adds spurious distractors. The dominant error pattern is systematic over-selection: the model selects an average of 6.22 options per question versus the gold standard of 4.44, a bias of nearly +1.78 options per question. This over-selection inflates recall (macro recall ~0.893) at the cost of precision (macro precision ~0.675) and nearly eliminates exact-match accuracy. The model rarely misses correct answers but routinely includes incorrect ones, suggesting it lacks confidence in distinguishing 'malicious-level' evidence from 'suspicious' or 'informative' evidence — a critical nuance in sandbox report interpretation. Performance is strongest on questions requiring broad enumeration (Q1 Evasion Techniques, Q8 Network IOC Extraction) where the gold answer set is large and the model's over-selection is less penalized. Performance is weakest on Behavioral Analysis and Process Injection Detection topics, where precise threat-level filtering (e.g., distinguishing threat_level:2 from threat_level:1 or informative signatures) is required. The remcos family is the weakest attack family (0% exact match, avg Jaccard 0.526). The killers family shows the best Jaccard despite 0% exact match, suggesting the model identifies most correct items but still over-selects. The single medium-difficulty question was solved perfectly; all hard questions show poor exact-match performance. Key training priorities: (1) Questions requiring strict threat-level filtering (malicious vs suspicious vs informative), (2) Behavioral Analysis questions across all attack families, (3) Process Injection Detection questions requiring precise API-level evidence grounding, (4) Remcos RAT questions specifically, (5) Questions with small gold answer sets (2-3 options) where over-selection is most penalized.

weaknesses.description: The model selects an average of 6.22 options versus 4.44 gold options (+1.78 bias). It consistently includes options classified at threat_level:1 (suspicious) or informative when the question specifically asks for threat_level:2 (malicious) findings. This is the primary driver of near-zero exact-match accuracy.
